assignments.name: translation_prompt
assignments.value:
=You are generating LinkedIn job-search keywords from a career title.

Career title: "{{ $json.raw_career_title }}"
Alternative titles for the same career (the report's other framings, already searched directly): {{ $json.alt_titles_summary }}
What the role actually does (plain-English overview, 25-40 words): {{ $json.career_overview || 'not provided' }}

Produce 1-2 short search phrases a recruiter would actually use as a LinkedIn job title to find this kind of role.

Rules:
- PRESERVE the core specialization, especially domain terms: "AI", "Artificial Intelligence", "Machine Learning", "ML", "Data", plus the core function (e.g. "Automation", "Transformation", "Lecturer", "Consultant").
- DROP seniority/engagement modifiers: "Part-Time", "Interim", "Fractional", "Senior", "Principal", "Junior", "independent", "Executive" (unless it is the actual role noun like "Account Executive").
- DROP trailing qualifiers like "for Business", "at a startup", "(Non-Equity)".
- LEVERAGE the alt titles above as context — they're real-world labels for the same target role. Your output keywords can echo one if it is genuinely the most LinkedIn-natural search term, or propose complementary variations that broaden the net. Aim for the best LinkedIn search coverage.
- Keep each phrase 2-3 words, natural as a real LinkedIn job title. Phrases must be in English.
- If the role is genuinely AI-focused, at least one phrase MUST contain "AI".

Return ONLY this JSON, no other text:
{"keywords":["...","..."]}

assignments.name: scoring_prompt
assignments.value:
=You are scoring LinkedIn job listings for relevance to a user's recommended career path.

User's recommended career: "{{ $json.careerTitle }}"
Alternative acceptable titles for the same career (treat as equivalent match targets): {{ $json.alt_titles_summary }}
What this role actually does (plain-English, 25-40 words): {{ $json.career_overview || 'not provided' }}
User's languages (with proficiency): {{ $json.languages_summary }}

Score each job 0-10 based on how well it matches this career path:
- 10 = direct match in role, level, and domain
- 7-9 = strong match (same domain, similar role level)
- 4-6 = adjacent (related skills or seniority but different focus)
- 0-3 = unrelated role, wrong domain, or junior/senior mismatch

Consider: role type (e.g. board/coach/leader/individual contributor), seniority level, domain (people/culture, product, engineering, sales, etc.), and whether the job sensibly aligns with the career path described.

ALT-TITLES RULE: The recommended career has alternative acceptable titles (listed above) that are different real-world labels for the SAME target role. A job that closely matches ANY one of them (in role function + domain) MUST be scored as if it matched the primary career — do not dock it for using a different label. Primary and alternatives are equally valid match targets. If the alt titles value is "none", ignore this rule.

SPECIALIZATION RULE (critical): The career has a core specialization — usually a domain like "AI / Machine Learning" plus a function. If a job does NOT clearly involve that core specialization (e.g. a generic "Software Engineer" with no AI/ML focus when the career is "AI Workflow Automation Engineer", or a generic "Transformation Lead" with no AI focus), CAP its score at 3 even if seniority and general field match. We would much rather show the user nothing, or an honestly low-scored match, than a confident-looking result that misses the whole point of their target role. Do not be generous.

AVOID-PREFERENCES RULE: The user explicitly told us to AVOID these industries / role aspects / environments: {{ $json.avoid_summary }}. If a job clearly matches one or more of them, apply a SIGNIFICANT penalty — drop 3-5 points, and cap the score at 2 for a strong match (e.g. a sales or client-facing role when "Sales" is on the avoid list). Briefly note the conflict in the reason. If the value is "none specified", ignore this rule.

Language-fit rule:
- If a job's title or description implies a language requirement (e.g. 'Native Dutch', 'Fluent German', a job description written in a language the user doesn't speak at conversational+), apply a SIGNIFICANT score penalty (typically drop 3-5 points) because the user couldn't realistically take the role.
- If the user's languages are 'unknown — assume English only', treat non-English jobs as language-restricted.
- Briefly note language fit in the reason when it materially affects the score.

ROLE-FUNCTION RULE: A consultant/advisor role (external, multi-client) is NOT the same as an in-house architect or platform owner (single-employer, internal). Same distinction applies to IC vs manager, hands-on vs strategy-only. When the role function clearly differs even though the domain overlaps, CAP the score at 5.

SENIORITY RULE: If the career title implies senior level (Principal, Head of, Director, Lead, VP, Chief, Fractional, Interim), and the job description specifies a years-of-experience range below 8 OR describes itself as "Mid-level" or "Associate", CAP at 4. When the description doesn't explicitly state seniority, treat the title alone as ambiguous and score no higher than 6.

BIAS TO STRICTNESS: When uncertain between two scores, pick the lower one. False high scores ("confident wrong") are worse than missing a marginal match.

Return ONLY valid JSON in this exact shape, no other text or commentary:
{"scores":[{"i":0,"score":8,"reason":"Director-level people role at scale-up — strong fit"}]}

Reason must be max 14 words, concrete (cite domain/level + language fit if relevant), no fluff.

Jobs to score:
{{ JSON.stringify($json.jobList, null, 2) }}
